data.title: Columbia Banking System Non-GAAP EPS of $0.76 beats by $0.03, revenue of $677M misses by $10.38M
data.description:
• Columbia Banking System press release (COLB): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.76 beats by $0.03.

data.title: Mattel expands WWE lineup with Lucha Libra action figures
data.description:
• Mattel (MAT) has expanded its World Wrestling Entertainment ((WWE)) (TKO) portfolio with Mexico’s Lucha Libre to launch a new line of AAA action figures inspired by the promotion’s legendary stars.
  • Beginning in the fall of 2027, Mattel’s (MAT) Lucha Libre AAA collection will expand on its WWE Elite Collection with AAA’s most recognizable talent, including Psycho Clown, El Hijo del Viking, La Parka, and Mr. Iguana.
  • To commemorate the announcement, Mattel Creations will debut a made-to-order Mr. Iguana action figure at San Diego Comic-Con, marking the first product in the new AAA collection.
  • In August 2025, WWE acquired AAA in partnership with Mexico-based sports and entertainment holding company, Fillip.

data.title: Winpak Ltd. GAAP EPS of $0.57, revenue of $294.4M
data.description:
• Winpak Ltd. press release (WPK:CA): Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.57.
  • Revenue of $294.4M.
  • Net income attributable to equity holders of the Company (Earnings) for the second quarter of 2026 of $33.6 million advanced by 11.3 percent from the $30.2 million recorded in the corresponding quarter in 2025. The improvement in gross profit was a key factor, raising Earnings by $6.0 million. Conversely, foreign exchange lowered Earnings by $1.3 million. Furthermore, operating expenses subtracted $1.0 million from Earnings. In combination, all other factors decreased Earnings by $0.3 million.

data.title: Lockheed Martin raises 2026 outlook to $79.75B-$81.75B in sales, driven by munitions and F-35 ramps
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lockheed Martin (LMT) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Lockheed Martin's second quarter results were strong" and "we are raising our 2026 guidance," with "our backlog" at "a new all-time high, now $230 billion" and "nearly $3 billion in free cash flow" (Chairman, President & CEO James Taiclet).

  • "In late June, the Missile Defense Agency awarded a 7-year contract for $35 billion to quadruple production of THAAD interceptors"; the company also cited "a GMLRS production contract at $3 billion" and "a HIMARS award valued at up to $1.1 billion" (Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet).

  • Management highlighted additional demand signals and technology programs, including: "The U.S. Space Force selected us as one of the awardees to develop space-based interceptor prototypes under Golden Dome with capability demonstration expected by 2028," plus "a $2.3 billion radar contract" and "cumulative contract modifications totaling $1.4 billion" on Conventional Prompt Strike (Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet).

  • Operational execution updates included, "We resumed deliveries for the F-16 and increased output of C-130 aircraft" and "Two F-16s and 7 C-130s were delivered to customers this quarter" (Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet).

  • M&A and partnership developments included, "we signed an agreement to acquire Ultra Maritime," and "our recent collaboration with General Motors Defense" to "explore applying the automotive industry's high-rate manufacturing and supply chain expertise to defense production" (Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet).

  • "We recorded sales of $20.1 billion" and "earnings per share rose to $7.94" while "free cash flow" was "a positive $2.9 billion this quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Evan Scott).

Outlook

  • "We are raising our outlook across every key metric" and now expect "total sales of $79.75 billion to $81.75 billion" and "earnings per share...$29.95 to $30.65" (CFO Scott).

  • Profit and cash guidance were also increased to "segment operating profit...$8.5 billion to $8.7 billion" and "free cash flow...$7 billion to $7.2 billion" (CFO Scott).

  • CapEx guidance shifted lower, with "capital expenditure guidance" updated to "$2 billion to $2.4 billion," which management tied to "efficiencies realized in MFC's munitions build-out" while saying "Expanding munitions capacity as quickly as possible is our top priority" (CFO Scott).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of guidance as unchanged ("Our 2026 financial outlook remains consistent" in Q1) and a free cash flow range of "$6.5 billion to $6.8 billion" (Senior VP & CFO Evan Scott, Q1), management in Q2 emphasized "accelerating" growth and a higher full-year trajectory (CFO Scott, Q2).

Financial Results

  • "We recorded a second quarter book-to-bill ratio of 3.2:1" and "record-setting" backlog of "$230 billion"; the company also cited "$65 billion of orders realized during the quarter" (CFO Scott).

  • The company reported profitability and margin figures: "segment operating margin was 10.8%" and "profit climbed to $2.2 billion" alongside "earnings per share...$7.94" (CFO Scott).

  • Cash flow inflection vs. last year was attributed to timing and taxes: "Free cash flow rose by $3 billion, moving from a negative $150 million...to a positive $2.9 billion," driven by "favorable timing of customer receipts" and "lower tax payments" (CFO Scott).

  • Segment guidance changes and notable offsets included: "Margins are pegged modestly lower than our prior guidance" in Aeronautics as it scales F-35 work and "absorb[s] the F-16 and C-130 challenges we realized in Q1" (Vice President of Investor Relations Mark Kvasnak). In Space, "profit outlook was lowered...due to reduced ULA equity earnings because of the ongoing technical investigation of the Vulcan launch anomaly" (VP Kvasnak).

Q&A

  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: asked about manufacturing scale-up timelines and willingness to invest ahead of awards; Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet said, "we are investing in the manufacturing and design capabilities before the orders come in" as part of a "mindset change" to be "America's clear leader in the Defense Technology segment."

  • Scott Mikus, Melius Research: asked whether a commercial acquisition push will be durable; Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet said multiyear frameworks are meant to be "long-term enforceable agreements and contracts that can't be changed at the whim of the government" and added, "We're not taking that kind of risk without a counterparty that's willing to work on a commercial basis right along with us."

  • John Godyn, Citi: raised investor confidence concerns around revenue acceleration; Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet cited F-35 demand and said, "we feel really confident that the 156 per year production rate will be sustained for quite some time"; CFO Scott added that as frameworks convert, "we're intending in the second half of the year to also get a multiyear on PAC-3."

  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: asked about risks on classified programs and broader portfolio risk; CFO Scott said the last adjustments were "in 4Q of 2024" (MFC) and "2Q of 2025" (Aeronautics) and added, "we set new baselines" and "have shown stability in our ability to execute," while emphasizing scaling and "resilience" (including the F-16 redesign and resumed deliveries).

  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: questioned CapEx efficiency and funding into 2027; CFO Scott said the "commitment to invest a total between $8 billion to $9 billion of capital in munition scaling is unchanged," and Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet described using partners to avoid new facilities, citing plans to "move that ATACMS line into factory space in Germany" to expand PrSM capacity.

  • Robert Stallard, Vertical: asked about initial THAAD margins; CFO Scott said, "We expect it to be consistent with our historic munitions production margins" while noting potential "near-term dilution" as profit rates step up with milestones.

  • Matthew Akers, BNP Paribas: asked about longer-term MFC margins; CFO Scott said MFC margins should be "in kind of the high 13s, low 14s" with possible "near-term dilution in the 20 to 30 basis points" and "a goal to get to higher than historic margins on MFC"; Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet argued frameworks change incentives because "as Evan suggested, if we get efficiencies...we get to keep in our own profitability."

  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: asked what changes when UCAS convert to multiyears; CFO Scott said UCAS "allow us to get going at full speed" and conversion will finalize "delivery schedules" and "profit mechanisms," while stating, "with the UCAS, we have all we need for now."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing in tone around durability of acquisition reform and investor confidence, including "the stock price...suggests a lot of concern" (John Godyn, Citi) and questions probing risk and reversals (Scott Mikus, Melius Research).

  • Management tone was positive and assertive in prepared remarks ("Consequently, we are raising our 2026 guidance") and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing mindset and contracting structure; examples included "we are investing...before the orders come in" and "we're intending...to also get a multiyear on PAC-3" (Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet; CFO Scott).

  • Versus Q1, management language shifted from holding the line ("Our 2026 financial outlook remains consistent," Q1) to stronger confidence signaling ("raising our outlook across every key metric," Q2), while Q2 Q&A featured more explicit engagement with stock sentiment and contracting durability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture changed from Q1 maintaining ranges ("remains consistent") to Q2 increasing key metrics ("raising our outlook across every key metric") and explicitly describing a stronger second-half growth setup (Senior VP & CFO Scott, Q1; CFO Scott, Q2).

  • The munitions framework narrative progressed from Q1 updates on awards and definitization efforts ("We are partnering...to definitize") to Q2 highlighting a major converted contract ("7-year contract for $35 billion") and the next expected conversion ("intending in the second half of the year...a multiyear on PAC-3") (CFO Scott; Chairman, President & CEO Taiclet).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q1 concentration on F-35 orientation and munitions ramp risk to Q2 emphasis on commercial-acquisition durability, stock-price skepticism, and contract economics/margins under frameworks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Execution and scaling risk remained a recurring theme as management described being "in a scaling mode right now" and framed key tests as "can we scale and can we be resilient" (CFO Scott).

  • Space profit headwind was linked to ULA: "profit outlook was lowered...due to reduced ULA equity earnings because of the ongoing technical investigation of the Vulcan launch anomaly" (VP Kvasnak).

  • Margin cadence risk on large ramps was acknowledged: "there can be a little bit of near-term dilution" on THAAD as profit booking rates step up with milestones and deliveries (CFO Scott).

Final Takeaway

Lockheed Martin management framed Q2 as a step-change quarter, citing a "$230 billion" backlog, "$20.1 billion" in sales, "$7.94" EPS, and "$2.9 billion" free cash flow, and used that performance to justify raising full-year 2026 guidance across sales, profit, cash flow, and EPS. Leadership emphasized that multiyear munitions frameworks are translating into large contracts (including a "$35 billion" THAAD award) while pointing to additional conversions ahead, and positioned both capacity expansion and targeted M&A (Ultra Maritime) as enabled by cash generation and contracting structures designed to support faster production ramps.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Norfolk Southern updates 2026 operating expense outlook to $8.8B-$8.9B amid $400M-$500M fuel swing
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Norfolk Southern (NSC) Q2 2026 Earnings Call

Management view

  • "A lot has changed since our last call, most importantly, the sharp inflection in volumes." (President, CEO & Director Mark George) "Initially catalyzed by the Iran conflict that bolstered our energy markets and that strength has now spread into other markets, including domestic intermodal and industrial products."
  • "We delivered a strong second quarter with results that exceeded our own expectations starting with strong volume and revenue growth, culminating in 7% net income and EPS growth." (President, CEO & Director Mark George)
  • "Absorbing the higher volumes coming out of the winter disruptions put pressure on the network, but we've addressed these issues head on." (President, CEO & Director Mark George) "We already drove acceleration of the network here in July, and we will continue to progress."
  • "Now before we move on, I'd like to touch on the recent appointment of Brian as our Chief Operating Officer." (President, CEO & Director Mark George) "In his new role, he's building on the progress he himself helped us deliver."
  • "We remain committed to at least $150 million in cost takeout during 2026, which will deliver at least $650 million in cumulative savings over the 3-year period, exceeding our original target." (Chief Operating Officer Brian Barr)
  • "Even without fuel, we achieved record revenue in the quarter." (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Ed Elkins) "So overall, Intermodal revenue less fuel increased substantially by 7%... marking the beginning of a positive shift in intermodal pricing."
  • "We incurred $51 million in merger-related expenses during the quarter, while total costs related to the Eastern Ohio incident were $15 million." (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi) "Adjusting for these items, the operating ratio for the quarter was 65.5% and earnings per share was $3.52."

Outlook

  • "Our original OpEx guidance was $8.2 billion to $8.4 billion, which we are updating to account for the large swing in fuel estimated to be $400 million to $500 million of incremental expense." (President, CEO & Director Mark George) "So our new 2026 operating expense outlook is $8.8 billion to $8.9 billion."
  • "Neutralizing for the fuel impact, our core operating costs are trending toward the higher end of the prior range due to a stronger volume outlook." (President, CEO & Director Mark George)
  • "Our CapEx guidance of approximately $1.9 billion this year is unchanged." (President, CEO & Director Mark George) "We are maintaining discipline while continuing to invest in the safety, reliability and capacity of our network."
  • "Fuel price headwinds" were cited in Q1 as a key uncertainty (President, CEO & Director Mark George), while in Q2 the company explicitly raised the full-year operating expense outlook to reflect the fuel swing.

Financial results

  • "Adjusting for these items, the operating ratio for the quarter was 65.5% and earnings per share was $3.52." (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi)
  • "The operating ratio increased 210 basis points versus last year, with fuel price headwinds driving an approximate 110 basis point increase." (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi) "Inflationary pressures drove another 190 basis point headwind compared to last year."
  • "Costs were up 15%, over 2/3 of which was driven by the substantial rise in fuel expense this quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi)
  • "We incurred $6 million of restructuring costs." (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi)

Q&A

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: "can you talk about how we might see that sort of transition into pricing opportunity"; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "we are very optimistic for the outlook going forward... the opportunity to price in several key markets."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe: "do you feel like you need to add a lot of headcount"; President, CEO & Director George: "we do have pockets where we are a little bit tight on T&E" and "we probably have a little bit more hiring to do there."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe: "how to think about... cost and margin trends into Q3 and Q4"; Executive VP & CFO Zampi: "typical seasonality... flat to 50 basis points worse" but "we believe we can beat that normal sequential seasonality... up to 100 basis points better than normal."
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: "give us a sense in terms of how the cadence progresses"; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "we're solidly in a place where there's upward pressure" on highway pricing and "that will flow through over time into our long-term contracts."
  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen: "How should we think about the intermodal conversions"; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "Typically... an annual bid cycle" and the focus is "how do we deliver value... so that that 1-year commitment maybe turns into a generational commitment."
  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: "Has that ended at this point?" (re: merger-related share shift); President, CEO & Director George: "We did have some losses right out of the gate there" and "we'll largely be lapping that here in the fourth quarter."
  • David Vernon, Bernstein: "what you're seeing in the activity book"; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "we've seen a real acceleration in that pipeline" and "this goes beyond data centers."
  • Madison Pasterchick, Morgan Stanley: "any pull forward of import volumes"; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "we probably have seen a little bit of pull forward" but "it's probably too early" on incremental tariff-driven activity.
  • Stephanie Moore, Jefferies: "some uncertainty deal related"; President, CEO & Director George: "It's not paralyzing decision making. If anything, it's accelerating decision making."
  • Bascome Majors, Stephens: "merch plan compliance dropped"; Chief Operating Officer Barr: "Here right now in the third quarter, we're a 6.62% improvement currently from where we finished the second quarter."
  • Richa Talwar, Deutsche Bank: competitive products by an eastern peer; President, CEO & Director George: "we applaud them for their successes and we're really proud of ours."
  • Eric Morgan, Barclays: truck-cycle pass-through and mix; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Elkins: "We've taken that from a lag... down to a couple of quarters."
  • Ariel Rosa, Citigroup: "talk about the downside risk"; President, CEO & Director George: "higher fuel prices, sustained higher fuel prices eventually hurt the consumer" and "that is the risk."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts tone: slightly positive to probing, highlighted by "one of the more upbeat calls I've heard from you guys in a while" (Ariel Rosa, Citigroup), alongside pointed questions on service, costs, and competitive dynamics.
  • Management tone: slightly positive with caution, including "we remain optimistic about the demand environment" (President, CEO & Director Mark George) and "we're cautiously optimistic" (President, CEO & Director George).
  • Current vs. previous quarter: Q2 language emphasized a "sharp inflection in volumes" (President, CEO & Director George), while Q1 framed the macro as "a mix of puts and takes" (President, CEO & Director George).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership and operating narrative shifted from Q1’s PSR 2.0 framing under "John Orr, our Chief Operating Officer" (President, CEO & Director Mark George) to Q2’s announcement of "the recent appointment of Brian as our Chief Operating Officer" (President, CEO & Director George).
  • Guidance posture changed from Q1 "maintaining our current cost guidance" (President, CEO & Director Mark George) to Q2 raising the 2026 operating expense outlook to "$8.8 billion to $8.9 billion" (President, CEO & Director George), tied to a fuel swing.
  • Analyst focus broadened in Q2 toward intermodal pricing cadence, industrial development acceleration, and the sustainability of macro strength; Q1 questions leaned more toward weather impacts, intermodal competition post-merger announcement, and cost control mechanics.

Risks and concerns

  • "Energy prices, the consumer and interest rates all remain wildcards" (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Ed Elkins).
  • "Higher fuel prices, sustained higher fuel prices eventually hurt the consumer" (President, CEO & Director Mark George), with George also noting uncertainty around how long elevated prices persist.
  • Network execution risk was acknowledged: "recovering from several network disruptions while supporting that level of volume placed additional pressure on crew resources" (Chief Operating Officer Brian Barr).

Final takeaway

Management described Q2 as a demand-driven inflection with volume strength spreading beyond energy into domestic intermodal and industrial products, while emphasizing near-term network acceleration efforts, a raised 2026 operating expense outlook tied to fuel volatility, and continued progress on a proposed Union Pacific combination, alongside reiterated caution that sustained high fuel prices could ultimately pressure consumer demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: C&F Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $2.40, revenue of $40.8M
data.description:
• C&F Financial press release (CFFI): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.40.
  • Revenue of $40.8M.

  • Average loans increased $132.2 million, or 8.8 percent, for the second quarter of 2026 and increased $133.7 million, or 9.0 percent, for the first six months of 2026 compared to the same periods of 2025 due primarily to growth in the commercial real estate and land acquisition and development segments of the loan portfolio. Average deposits increased $149.4 million, or 6.7 percent, for the second quarter of 2026 and increased $164.9 million, or 7.5 percent, for the first six months of 2026 compared to the same periods of 2025 due primarily to higher balances of time deposits and savings and money market deposits. A portion of the increase in average deposits was due to the wind-down of the repurchase agreement program with certain commercial deposit customers during the third quarter of 2025. The average balance of those repurchase agreements was $23.9 million during the second quarter of 2025.

data.title: Globe Life projects 2026 net operating EPS of $15.55-$15.95 while lifting share repurchases to $670M-$700M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Globe Life (GL) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “In the second quarter, net income was $288 million or $3.65 per share... Net operating income for the quarter was $285 million or $3.61 per share” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Frank Svoboda).

  • “For the full year, we expect total premium revenue growth to be in the range of 6.5% to 7%” and “we anticipate life underwriting margin to be between 43% and 45% for the full year 2026... we do expect it to be over 50% in the third quarter due to the anticipated impact of assumption updates” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda).

  • “DTC is in a transition period due to a shift in the way consumers search online... The increased utilization of AI by consumers has resulted in a reduction in paid search volume... We have initiatives underway to adapt to this change” and “we still anticipate that we will meet agency demands by generating an excess of 1 million leads this year” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO James Darden).

  • “During the quarter, the company repurchased approximately 1.1 million shares... for a total cost of $175 million” and “for the full year... share repurchases will be in the range of $670 million to $700 million” (Executive VP & CFO Thomas Kalmbach).

Outlook

  • “For the full year 2026, we estimate net operating earnings per diluted share will be in the range of $15.55 to $15.95” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • “The guidance range reflects potential remeasurement gains from the third quarter life and health assumption updates in the range of $110 million to $130 million” and “we anticipate third quarter life underwriting margin as a percent of premium will be in the range of 52% to 53%” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • “For the full year, we expect total premium revenue growth to be in the range of 6.5% to 7%... we expect life premium revenue to grow between 2.5% and 3%... we expect health premium revenue to grow in the range of 14% to 16%” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s guidance language, management increased its 2026 net operating EPS range (from “$15.40 to $15.90” to “$15.55 to $15.95”) and increased expected share repurchases (from “$560 million to $610 million” to “$670 million to $700 million”) (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

Financial results

  • “Life premium revenue for the second quarter increased 3%... to $861 million... Health insurance, premium revenue grew 16% to $437 million” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda).

  • “Excess investment income... was $38 million... Net investment income was $294 million” and “invested assets are $22.1 billion” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda).

  • “For the second quarter, life policy obligations as a percent of premium improved... to 34.3%... Health obligations as a percent of premium were 56.8%... higher than our estimates” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • “The higher health obligation ratio was driven by a number of factors, including Medicare supplement claims related to prior periods... higher loss ratios at Evry... and... cancer claims at Liberty National division. We expect the claims experience to moderate during the remainder of the year” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

Q&A

  • Wilma Jackson Burdis, Raymond James: Asked how DTC advertising adapts to AI search and timing; Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden: “the quantity of search going down in the volume of paid search... bidding up the price... we’re going to be disciplined on our spend and make sure that we maintain our margin.”

  • Wilma Jackson Burdis, Raymond James: Asked about buyback pace; Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach: “we would anticipate the parent will return to shareholders... approximately $350 million to $370 million... share repurchases in that $670 million to $700 million range.”

  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: Asked about Bermuda cession capital and timing; Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach: “we don’t really expect any capital benefit in... 2026... and we’d expect to see some benefit in 2027... over a longer period of time... the next 3 to 5 years.”

  • Wes Carmichael, Wells Fargo: Asked if higher share price changes 2027 capital deployment and M&A; Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda: “the higher share price doesn’t deter us... we will look at... M&A opportunities... but in the meantime... we feel very comfortable that the current share price is still below what we think is the intrinsic value.”

  • Joel Hurwitz, Dowling: Pressed on AIL life sales coming in below outlook; Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden: “I do think it is agent count driven... we anticipate that Q3 and Q4... to be in that mid-single-digit growth rate.”

  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: Asked whether assumption update is a one-time benefit; Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach: “I would expect some remeasurement gains continue to come through.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, focusing on uncertainties in DTC (“how long you see it playing out?”) and health claims vs. higher assumption-update expectations (“I guess I’m a little surprised...”) (Wilma Jackson Burdis, Raymond James; Ryan Krueger, KBW).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, emphasizing discipline and continuity, including “we’re going to be disciplined on our spend... maintain our margin” and “we expect the claims experience to moderate” (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden; Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • Versus last quarter, management’s tone shifted toward addressing AI-driven disruption in online acquisition (“paid search has moved to AI-generated search”), while keeping a similar confidence level on capital return and assumption-update benefits (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden; Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Svoboda).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 placed heavier emphasis on DTC disruption from AI search and platform economics, compared with Q1’s emphasis on scaling lead generation and improving conversion from DTC leads to agencies (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden).

  • Capital return expectations increased, with Q2 adding term-loan expansion details (“increasing the principal balance from $250 million to $450 million”) and a higher repurchase range, versus Q1’s focus on accelerating buybacks due to “favorable market conditions” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • Bermuda framing moved from process updates (“anticipate filing for a reciprocal jurisdiction in the second quarter”) to regulatory progress and near-term execution (“Nebraska approved reciprocal jurisdiction... expect to complete the new reinsurance cession in the third quarter”), while still anchoring parent cash impacts primarily to 2027 (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

Risks and concerns

  • “DTC is in a transition period... reduction in paid search volume... bidding up the price for paid search” with management response focused on channel testing and spend discipline (Co-Chairman & Co-CEO Darden).

  • “Health obligations... higher than our estimates” tied to prior-period MedSup items, Evry volatility, and Liberty National cancer claims, with mitigation framed as moderation and annual assumption updates in Q3 (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

  • Regulatory timing risk around Bermuda dividends remained explicit: “the Bermuda Monetary Authority would need to approve those distributions to the parent” (Executive VP & CFO Kalmbach).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as continued earnings growth with raised 2026 net operating EPS guidance and higher planned share repurchases funded in part by increased parent liquidity, while highlighting two near-term swing factors investors were pressing on: the DTC shift from paid search to AI-influenced discovery and a higher-than-expected health claims quarter. Executives emphasized margin discipline in digital acquisition, expected moderation in health claims, and a Q3 assumption-update benefit that management indicated could be followed by ongoing quarterly remeasurement variability and a longer-run shift in baseline underwriting profitability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Heritage Financial outlines $64M-$65M Q3 noninterest expense and $56M-$57M Q4 run-rate as Kitsap conversion nears
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Heritage Financial Corporation (HFWA) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "The integration with Kitsap Bank is progressing as planned. We are converting systems late September and will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion." (CEO, President & Director Bryan McDonald)
  • "The second quarter net interest margin increased 3 basis points to 3.99% or 8 basis points if you adjust out the interest recovery that contributed to a higher margin in the first quarter. We expect the upward trajectory to continue but at a more moderate pace, primarily driven by new loans and repricing within the existing loan portfolio." (CEO, President & Director Bryan McDonald)
  • "Total loan balances increased $26 million in the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "Total deposits decreased $210 million in Q2 due to the seasonal decline that occurred in April related to tax payments and a $67 million decrease from a single deposit relationship..." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "Based on our current forecast of staffing levels and merger-related costs, we are remaining consistent with our guidance from last quarter in that we're expecting quarterly noninterest expense levels to be in the $64 million to $65 million range in Q3 before decreasing to a range of $56 million to $57 million in Q4." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "Nonaccrual loans totaled $15.5 million at quarter end, increasing by a modest $500,000 during the quarter." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Tony Chalfant)

Outlook

  • "Based on the current pipeline, we expect our annualized loan growth rate to be in the mid-single-digit range for the next couple of quarters." (CEO, President & Director Bryan McDonald)
  • "We expect the upward trajectory to continue but at a more moderate pace" for net interest margin. (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "We are remaining consistent with our guidance from last quarter" on noninterest expense: "$64 million to $65 million" in Q3 and "$56 million to $57 million" in Q4. (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Financial Results

  • "Net interest margin increased to 3.99% from 3.96% in the prior quarter and from 3.51% in the second quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "We recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $921,000 in Q2." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "During the quarter, we executed a small loss trade in which we sold $38 million of securities at a pretax loss of $217,000 and reinvested the proceeds into higher-yielding securities." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "During Q2, we repurchased 372,000 shares of common stock totaling $10 million." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "Total charge-offs remained low at $269,000... leading to net charge-offs of $234,000 for the quarter." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Tony Chalfant)

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: "Can you just quantify the merger charges you expect in 3Q and in 4Q..."; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "Q4... $56 million to $57 million... your run rate" and for Q3 merger charges "I'm guessing we've got another $6 million there."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: on FHLB borrowings; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "It's just as needed for liquidity purposes... If we get some nice deposit growth in Q3 as we normally do, I would expect those borrowing balances to decrease."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: on deposit costs; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "I think we hit the bottom... Our spot rate for interest-bearing deposits was 1.64% at the end of the quarter" and "we will see some gradual increases in cost of interest-bearing deposits from here on."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: on cost bridge and tail; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "Correct" and on further savings: "Very little. Not enough to really give you guidance on."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: on margin path; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "we're going to keep moving forward on the margin, but it won't be as strong as it was the prior quarter."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: on origination jump and competition; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "it's an increase in loan demand" and "we do regularly compete on price."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: on levers to defend margin; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "there's still significant upside in the margin from asset repricing" and "the loan-to-deposit ratio is also a really good opportunity to drive continued margin growth."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: on disruption and hiring; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "We're still out actively talking to talent" and "I see that strategy continuing."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc., Research Division: on synergy realization; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "we're on the cost savings that we're going to be hitting that on from the merger."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc., Research Division: on deposit growth expectations and competitiveness; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "mid-single-digit annualized growth type of thing" and "We are going to be -- we'll be competitive on rates for deposits."
  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: on balance sheet actions; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "we'll continue to be open to buybacks" and on securities: "We'll continue to look at... We don't have anything large planned at this time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on expenses and synergy capture, including "it feels like you're maybe coming up a little bit shy" (Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc., Research Division) and continued probing on merger-cost quantification and funding costs.
  • Management: neutral to slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and margin trend, including "progressing as planned" (CEO, President & Director McDonald) and "credit quality remained strong and stable" (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant), while acknowledging competitive funding pressure: "I think we've probably hit bottom" and "we will see some gradual increases" (Executive VP & CFO Hinson).
  • Versus prior quarter: management kept similar framing on margin trajectory and integration timing, while Q2 responses placed more emphasis on deposit competition and the idea that deposit costs could rise from a stated bottom.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Integration messaging stayed consistent, with Q2 repeating the same conversion timing and cost posture: "converting systems late September" and "carrying higher expenses until after the conversion" (CEO, President & Director McDonald) versus Q1: "we are converting systems in late September" and "we will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion" (CEO, President & Director McDonald).
  • Expense guidance language tightened around a Q4 "run rate" in Q2, including "Q4... $56 million to $57 million range... your run rate" (Executive VP & CFO Hinson), compared with Q1’s inclusion of an ex-merger framing: "If you take out merger costs, we're more in the $57 million to $58 million range" (Executive VP & CFO Hinson).
  • Loan production discussion shifted from lower Q1 closures to a strong Q2 rebound: "closed $339 million in new loan commitments, up from $166 million last quarter" (CEO, President & Director McDonald), while keeping the loan-growth outlook consistent: "mid-single-digit range" (CEO, President & Director McDonald) in both quarters.
  • Deposit-cost tone shifted from Q1 stability expectations ("I really don't expect it to move a whole lot" (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)) to Q2’s view that costs likely bottomed with gradual upward pressure ("we will see some gradual increases" (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Total deposits decreased $210 million in Q2" and management tied the move to seasonality, a single relationship decline, and brokered CD runoff. (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "there's a lot more competition for deposits" and "CD rates are starting to increase the competition" with an expectation of upward pressure on deposit costs. (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "the challenges in the economy have led to some pressure on certain segments of our C&I portfolio" even as management said "the risk has been manageable." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Tony Chalfant)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized late-September systems conversion execution, guided quarterly noninterest expense to $64 million-$65 million in Q3 and $56 million-$57 million in Q4, and described a continued—but more moderate—net interest margin uptrend after a 3.99% Q2 margin. Executives highlighted stronger loan commitment volumes alongside elevated prepayments, framed deposit outflows as largely seasonal and relationship-specific, and acknowledged rising competitive pressure on deposit pricing after stating interest-bearing deposit costs likely bottomed. Credit metrics were described as stable, with low net charge-offs and a small increase in nonaccrual loans.
